methods.rationale: 8 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Terrestrial Energy is a public-stage nuclear energy company that has raised $280M to date. The nuclear energy sector comprises 11 identifiable comparable companies: 3 publicly traded peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private competitors including Oklo, Nano Nuclear Energy, TerraPower, X-Energy, and Kairos Power.

The peer-median method proved inapplicable to this valuation. The nuclear energy universe contains only 2 same-stage comparables and zero adjacent-stage or similar-tier peers meeting minimum threshold criteria (≥3 datapoints required). While 8 same-sector peers exist, narrow-filter segmentation did not yield sufficient depth for reliable median derivation. A broad-sector median would introduce material distortion given the heterogeneity of nuclear technology approaches and development timelines across the peer set.

Without sufficient peer clustering, traditional public market multiples and private round pricing cannot be triangulated with confidence. The analysis indicates that Terrestrial Energy's valuation requires custom modeling anchored to technology-specific milestones, regulatory pathway clarity, and capital intensity rather than peer benchmarking. Key uncertainties include commercialization timelines for advanced reactor designs, regulatory approval risk, and working capital demands through deployment phases. Practitioners should rely on discounted cash flow or venture capital method frameworks supplemented by the 11-company sector landscape rather than multiple-based approaches.
